pillars.name: Molecular Biophysics
pillars.description: The physics of biological macromolecules: protein structure and folding, molecular motors, and the forces and energetics that govern molecular machines.
pillars.descriptionCs: Fyzika biologických makromolekul: struktura a sbalování bílkovin, molekulární motory a síly a energetika řídící molekulární stroje.

blocks.name: Protein Structure & Folding
blocks.description: Proteins fold from linear chains into specific three-dimensional shapes that determine their function, driven toward a free-energy minimum by hydrophobic, electrostatic and hydrogen-bonding forces; misfolding underlies several diseases.
blocks.descriptionCs: Bílkoviny se sbalují z lineárních řetězců do specifických trojrozměrných tvarů určujících jejich funkci, vedeny k minimu volné energie hydrofobními, elektrostatickými a vodíkovými vazbami; chybné sbalení je základem několika onemocnění.

brief.name: Protein folding
brief.definition: The process by which a polypeptide chain adopts its functional three-dimensional structure.

brief.name: Free-energy landscape
brief.definition: The funnel of conformational energies guiding a protein to its native state.

brief.name: Hydrophobic effect
brief.definition: The tendency of nonpolar groups to cluster away from water, driving folding.

brief.name: Conformational change
brief.definition: A functional shape change of a protein in response to binding or signals.

brief.name: Misfolding
brief.definition: Incorrect folding producing aggregates linked to disease.

blocks.name: Molecular Motors & Machines
blocks.description: Molecular motors convert chemical energy into directed motion and force, walking along filaments or rotating within membranes; they power muscle contraction, intracellular transport and the synthesis of the cell's energy currency.
blocks.descriptionCs: Molekulární motory přeměňují chemickou energii na řízený pohyb a sílu, kráčejí podél filament nebo rotují v membránách; pohánějí svalový stah, nitrobuněčný transport a syntézu energetické měny buňky.

brief.name: Molecular motor
brief.definition: A protein converting chemical energy into mechanical work.

brief.name: ATP hydrolysis
brief.definition: The energy-releasing reaction powering most molecular motors.

brief.name: Cytoskeletal transport
brief.definition: The motor-driven movement of cargo along filaments within cells.

brief.name: Brownian ratchet
brief.definition: A mechanism rectifying random thermal motion into directed movement.

brief.name: Rotary motor
brief.definition: A membrane machine such as the one synthesising the cell's energy currency by rotation.

pillars.name: Membrane & Neural Biophysics
pillars.description: The physics of cell membranes and excitable cells: ion transport and membrane potentials, the action potential, and the electrical signalling of nerves.
pillars.descriptionCs: Fyzika buněčných membrán a dráždivých buněk: transport iontů a membránové potenciály, akční potenciál a elektrická signalizace nervů.

blocks.name: Membrane Potentials & Ion Transport
blocks.description: Cell membranes maintain electrical potentials by selectively transporting ions through channels and pumps; the resulting gradients store energy and set the resting potential from which excitable cells generate signals.
blocks.descriptionCs: Buněčné membrány udržují elektrické potenciály selektivním transportem iontů skrze kanály a pumpy; vzniklé gradienty ukládají energii a určují klidový potenciál, z něhož dráždivé buňky vytvářejí signály.

brief.name: Membrane potential
brief.definition: The voltage difference across a cell membrane.

brief.name: Ion channel
brief.definition: A membrane protein allowing specific ions to flow down their gradient.

brief.name: Ion pump
brief.definition: A protein using energy to move ions against their gradient.

brief.name: Nernst potential
brief.definition: The equilibrium voltage for an ion set by its concentration ratio.

brief.name: Resting potential
brief.definition: The steady membrane voltage of an unstimulated cell.

blocks.name: The Action Potential & Nerve Signalling
blocks.description: Excitable cells fire action potentials — rapid, self-propagating reversals of membrane potential driven by voltage-gated ion channels — which carry electrical signals along nerves, as captured by the Hodgkin-Huxley model.
blocks.descriptionCs: Dráždivé buňky generují akční potenciály — rychlé, samovolně se šířící obrácení membránového potenciálu poháněná napěťově řízenými iontovými kanály — které přenášejí elektrické signály podél nervů, jak popisuje Hodgkinův–Huxleyův model.

brief.name: Action potential
brief.definition: A rapid all-or-nothing reversal of membrane potential that propagates along a cell.

brief.name: Voltage-gated channel
brief.definition: An ion channel that opens in response to membrane voltage.

brief.name: Hodgkin-Huxley model
brief.definition: The quantitative model of the action potential from ionic currents.

brief.name: Saltatory conduction
brief.definition: The jumping of nerve impulses between gaps in the myelin sheath, speeding signalling.

brief.name: Synaptic transmission
brief.definition: The passing of a signal between cells at a synapse.

pillars.name: Biomechanics
pillars.description: The mechanics of living matter: the material properties of tissues, the forces in movement and locomotion, and fluid flow in circulation and respiration.
pillars.descriptionCs: Mechanika živé hmoty: materiálové vlastnosti tkání, síly při pohybu a lokomoci a proudění tekutin v oběhu a dýchání.

blocks.name: Tissue Mechanics & Locomotion
blocks.description: Biomechanics applies the mechanics of materials and forces to living tissue: bones, muscles and tendons carry loads and store elastic energy, and the physics of levers and gaits governs how organisms move.
blocks.descriptionCs: Biomechanika aplikuje mechaniku materiálů a sil na živou tkáň: kosti, svaly a šlachy nesou zatížení a ukládají pružnou energii a fyzika pák a chůze určuje, jak se organismy pohybují.

brief.name: Tissue elasticity
brief.definition: The stress-strain response of biological materials such as bone and tendon.

brief.name: Muscle mechanics
brief.definition: The force-length and force-velocity behaviour of contracting muscle.

brief.name: Lever systems
brief.definition: The skeletal arrangements of bones and muscles acting as levers.

brief.name: Gait
brief.definition: The mechanical pattern of legged locomotion optimising energy and stability.

brief.name: Scaling laws
brief.definition: How mechanical demands change with organism size.

blocks.name: Biological Fluid Dynamics
blocks.description: The flow of biological fluids follows the physics of hydrodynamics adapted to living systems: blood circulates through branching vessels, air moves through the lungs, and microorganisms swim at low Reynolds number where viscosity dominates.
blocks.descriptionCs: Proudění biologických tekutin se řídí fyzikou hydrodynamiky přizpůsobenou živým systémům: krev obíhá větvícími se cévami, vzduch proudí plícemi a mikroorganismy plavou při nízkém Reynoldsově čísle, kde převládá viskozita.

brief.name: Haemodynamics
brief.definition: The physics of blood flow through the circulatory system.

brief.name: Poiseuille flow
brief.definition: The pressure-driven laminar flow of fluid through a tube.

brief.name: Low Reynolds number
brief.definition: The viscosity-dominated regime in which microorganisms swim.

brief.name: Respiratory flow
brief.definition: The mechanics of air movement in breathing.

brief.name: Wall shear stress
brief.definition: The frictional force of flowing fluid on vessel walls, sensed by cells.

pillars.name: Biophysical Techniques
pillars.description: The physical methods that image and probe living systems: structural determination, single-molecule and optical techniques, and the physics of medical imaging.
pillars.descriptionCs: Fyzikální metody zobrazující a zkoumající živé systémy: určování struktury, jednomolekulové a optické techniky a fyzika lékařského zobrazování.

blocks.name: Structural & Single-Molecule Methods
blocks.description: Physical techniques resolve biological structure and dynamics down to single molecules: X-ray crystallography, cryo-electron microscopy and nuclear magnetic resonance yield atomic structures, while optical tweezers and single-molecule fluorescence probe individual molecules at work.
blocks.descriptionCs: Fyzikální techniky rozlišují biologickou strukturu a dynamiku až na úroveň jednotlivých molekul: rentgenová krystalografie, kryoelektronová mikroskopie a nukleární magnetická rezonance dávají atomární struktury, zatímco optická pinzeta a jednomolekulová fluorescence zkoumají jednotlivé molekuly při práci.

brief.name: X-ray crystallography
brief.definition: Determining atomic structure from the diffraction of X-rays by a crystal.

brief.name: Cryo-electron microscopy
brief.definition: Imaging frozen biomolecules at near-atomic resolution by electron microscopy.

brief.name: Nuclear magnetic resonance
brief.definition: Probing molecular structure and dynamics through nuclear spin resonance.

brief.name: Optical tweezers
brief.definition: Trapping and manipulating single molecules or particles with focused light.

brief.name: Single-molecule fluorescence
brief.definition: Observing individual molecules by the light they emit.

blocks.name: Physics of Medical Imaging
blocks.description: Medical imaging applies physics to see inside the body without surgery: X-ray and computed tomography use ionising radiation, magnetic resonance imaging uses nuclear spins in magnetic fields, and ultrasound uses reflected sound waves.
blocks.descriptionCs: Lékařské zobrazování aplikuje fyziku k pohledu dovnitř těla bez operace: rentgen a výpočetní tomografie využívají ionizující záření, magnetická rezonance využívá jaderné spiny v magnetickém poli a ultrazvuk využívá odražené zvukové vlny.

brief.name: Computed tomography
brief.definition: Reconstruction of cross-sectional images from many X-ray projections.

brief.name: Magnetic resonance imaging
brief.definition: Imaging based on the nuclear magnetic resonance of tissue protons.

brief.name: Ultrasound imaging
brief.definition: Imaging from the echoes of high-frequency sound waves.

brief.name: Contrast mechanism
brief.definition: The physical property that distinguishes tissues in an image.

brief.name: Spatial resolution
brief.definition: The finest detail an imaging method can resolve.
